review_summary: This unit clearly supports questions about colorization as automatic cross-channel supervision and about temporal color propagation as a way to learn correspondences.

question_bank.question: In LAB colorization pretraining, what is the model typically asked to predict?
choices: The a and b color channels from the L lightness channel.
The L lightness channel from manually labeled object masks.
The class label from a fully colored RGB image.
The camera pose from a single grayscale pixel.
question_bank.explanation: LAB separates lightness from color. A colorization pretext task can use the lightness channel as input and the existing color channels as automatically available targets.

transcript_quotes: predict the A and B channels

question_bank.question: What broader idea does a split-brain autoencoder illustrate?
choices: A model can learn representations by predicting one set of channels from another set of channels.
A model must always predict labels created by humans.
A decoder is unnecessary because the encoder directly outputs class probabilities.
Only RGB color channels can ever be used for self-supervised learning.
question_bank.explanation: Split-brain style tasks generalize colorization into cross-channel prediction. The supervision comes from relationships between parts of the input signal itself.

question_bank.question: Why can video colorization lead to tracking-like representations?
choices: The model must find corresponding pixels or regions across frames to propagate colors consistently.
The model is explicitly given keypoint labels for every frame.
The model ignores temporal consistency and colorizes each frame independently.
The task only requires predicting the average color of the full video.
question_bank.explanation: To transfer color from a reference frame to later frames, the model has to match regions across time. That correspondence pressure can induce tracking of pixels, objects, or keypoints.
